serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Mutton Tea Leaf Salad is approximately 250 g, comprising cooked mutton pieces tossed with fermented tea leaves, fried garnishes, and fresh vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from USDA FoodData Central: Lamb, domestic, composite of trimmed retail cuts, cooked (NDB 17003) combined with component estimates for fermented tea leaf salad base (lahpet thoke) derived from FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data and peer-reviewed literature on Burmese lahpet thoke composition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Mutton Tea Leaf Salad exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central data for cooked lamb/mutton (NDB 17003, approximately 40% of dish weight) with component-level estimates for fermented tea leaves, fried peanuts, sesame seeds, dried shrimp, fried garlic, and fresh vegetables. The composite reflects a typical restaurant-style preparation. Values should be treated as estimates with medium confidence.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed meat and vegetable salad with legumes estimated from analogous low-GI components; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mutton Tea Leaf Salad. GI estimated at 32 based on the low-GI profile of the dish's dominant components: fermented tea leaves (negligible glycemic carbohydrates), cooked mutton (protein-dominant, GI effectively 0), fried legumes and peanuts (GI approximately 14-32 per University of Sydney GI Database), and modest carbohydrate content from vegetables and beans. The composite GI of approximately 32 is consistent with mixed meat-legume-vegetable salads in the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per serving = (32 × 7.2 × 2.5) / 100 = 5.76, rounded to 5.8.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce, dried shrimp, and fermented tea leaves
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a standard serving, as the estimated 480 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 1200 mg per full 250 g serving) represents over 50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; requesting reduced fish sauce and dried shrimp can meaningfully lower sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI 32) and moderate carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mutton Tea Leaf Salad has a low estimated glycemic index of 32 and a glycemic load of approximately 5.8 per serving, making it a suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in standard portions; the high protein content from mutton further attenuates postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from mutton and sodium from condiments
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish contains approximately 3.9 g saturated fat per 100 g primarily from mutton, and elevated sodium from fish sauce and dried shrimp; individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size and request preparation with reduced fish sauce, while benefiting from the cardioprotective unsaturated fats from sesame seeds and peanuts.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mutton Tea Leaf Salad (ဆိတ်လက်ဖက်ထောင်း, also known as Lahpet Thoke with Mutton) is a Burmese-origin dish served in Singapore that combines cooked mutton (goat meat) with fermented tea leaves (lahpet), the iconic ingredient of Burmese cuisine. The dish is assembled cold or at room temperature by tossing tender cooked mutton pieces with fermented and seasoned tea leaves, fried garlic, roasted peanuts, sesame seeds, dried shrimp, fried broad beans, fresh tomato, shredded cabbage, lime juice, fish sauce, and chilli. The result is a complex, layered flavour profile combining savoury, umami, sour, nutty, and mildly bitter notes from the fermented tea leaves. The texture is varied, with tender meat, crunchy fried garnishes, and soft fermented leaves. Nutritionally, the dish is protein-rich (approximately 14.5 g per 100 g) from the mutton, with moderate fat content (11.8 g per 100 g) including beneficial unsaturated fats from peanuts and sesame. The fermented tea leaves contribute polyphenols and dietary fibre. The primary clinical concern is sodium content (approximately 480 mg per 100 g) from fish sauce and dried shrimp, making portion control important for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The low glycemic index (estimated GI 32) makes it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. In Singapore, this dish is served at Burmese and Southeast Asian restaurants and represents a culturally significant preparation that bridges Burmese culinary tradition with the multicultural food landscape of Singapore.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Mutton Tea Leaf Salad exists in any approved primary regional source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for cooked lamb/mutton combined with component-level estimates for fermented tea leaves and garnishes. GI is estimated by analogy from University of Sydney GI Database entries for comparable low-GI mixed meat-legume-vegetable dishes. The cuisine tag in the input record was incorrectly listed as Italian; the dish is correctly identified as Burmese origin based on the Burmese-script name (ဆိတ်လက်ဖက်ထောင်း) and dish characteristics.
notes: 1. Input cuisine field was 'Italian' — corrected to 'Burmese' based on the Burmese-script dish name (ဆိတ်လက်ဖက်ထောင်း) and dish characteristics. The restaurant 'The Light Cafe - High Street Plaza' appears to serve a multicultural menu including Burmese dishes. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source data for cooked lamb/mutton and is not expected to be a significant contributor in this dish. 3. All other -1 values in per_100g have been replaced with estimated numeric values from component analysis. 4. Halal status is marked false as the dish contains dried shrimp and fish sauce which may not be halal-certified; verification with the specific restaurant is required. 5. The dish is served at a Singapore restaurant but is Burmese in origin; multilingual names follow the Singaporean dishes rule (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as this is a Singapore-served dish, with the Burmese script preserved in romanized_local and aliases. 6. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per serving = (32 × 7.2 × 250) / 10000 = 5.76, rounded to 5.8.
